title: The Michael Keaton Movie Mount Rushmore
format: On the eve of 'Beetlejuice Beetlejuice,' Sean and Amanda bring in Griffin Newman of Blank Check to argue Michael Keaton's whole strange career into four faces on a Mount Rushmore. Then Sean talks to Jason Schwartzman and Nathan Silver about 'Between the Temples.'

segments.heading: Michael Keaton, the strangest movie star
segments.note: Sean and Amanda with Griffin Newman of Blank Check

films.title: Beetlejuice
films.note: Griffin's gateway to Keaton, ahead of Batman. A PG movie with severed heads, rooted in German expressionism and borscht-belt comedy, where Keaton has under 20 minutes of screen time and doesn't appear for 38 minutes but owns the poster. The role Burton first imagined for Sammy Davis Jr.

films.title: Beetlejuice Beetlejuice
films.note: The reason for the episode, out the coming week. Keaton's fifth Burton film. Recorded hours after a four-minute standing ovation at its Venice premiere; the reassuring word from people who'd seen it was that it isn't weighed down by self-important legacy-sequel bullshit.

films.title: Batman
films.note: The left-turn casting that made fans furious and then converted them; to Sean, still the best Batman. The one time all eyes were on Keaton. He'd circle back for Batman Returns, The Flash, and the shelved Batgirl.

films.title: Batman Returns
films.note: One of his best movies, though it belongs to Michelle Pfeiffer and Danny DeVito. Their heat is the real engine; Amanda fast-forwarded to the scenes between Keaton and Pfeiffer and called it a great way to watch it.

films.title: Mr. Mom
films.note: The full-bore hit that codified his movie-star persona and later lands as the Jefferson slot. Written by John Hughes; watching someone else shoot a Hughes script, Griffin says, underlines how much Hughes did as a director. Kind of dopey, kind of good, all Keaton.

films.title: Night Shift
films.note: His first proper movie and a relative hit, co-lead in the Ron Howard comedy that launched the 'glib young man' persona.

films.title: Johnny Dangerously
films.note: Amy Heckerling's gangster spoof, Keaton doing a Cagney-meets-modern-meta thing. Excellent and a hard bomb; Griffin's John Adams of the filmography.

films.title: Gung Ho
films.note: Ron Howard's culturally cringey relic about a Japanese company taking over an American car plant. The first movie where he's reaching for a serious, Jimmy Stewart register; Gedde Watanabe is the standout.

films.title: Clean and Sober
films.note: His first real dramatic role, same year as Beetlejuice; the two got him a National Board of Review best-actor nod and helped sell Warner Bros. on Batman. Sean found it overwrought going back.

films.title: Pacific Heights
films.note: John Schlesinger's dingy yuppie-nightmare thriller, one of Scott Rudin's first productions. Keaton as an evil tenant against Melanie Griffith and Matthew Modine; entertaining, though you never quite learn what's in it for him.

films.title: One Good Cop
films.note: Griffin's pick for the worst film of his he's seen, and one of the rare cases where Keaton himself is bad and the whole movie is out of whack.

films.title: Much Ado About Nothing
films.note: A supporting Dogberry, and an interpretation of Shakespeare unlike any other actor's: the weirdness applied to the Bard.

films.title: My Life
films.note: Bruce Joel Rubin's flat, un-cinematic drama about a dying man taping videos for the son he won't live to see. Should be devastating; lands with the mise en scène of an episode of Mad About You.

films.title: The Paper
films.note: The noisy Ron Howard newspaper ensemble that should have unlocked his Hanks phase and comes up short. Griffin wanted to reclaim it and couldn't; it's a mess he adores anyway.

films.title: Speechless
films.note: A classic rom-com setup reuniting him with Geena Davis after Beetlejuice, one of four conventional mid-'90s leads that just don't live on.

films.title: Multiplicity
films.note: Harold Ramis high-concept comedy, his last strike at a comfortable classic-Keaton broad-comedy turn. When it didn't work, he throttled down for years.

films.title: Jackie Brown
films.note: One half of the great double Ray Nicolette gag: he plays the same ATF agent here and in Out of Sight. A cool, small performance from his slow-down years.

films.title: Out of Sight
films.note: The other Ray Nicolette. The crossover rules, but it's two small performances in a stretch where the gaps between his movies were widening.

films.title: Jack Frost
films.note: One of his lesser films (the source of the photo on Griffin's 'Michael Keaton Rules' shirt), and shorthand for the fallow periods when nobody cared and he was just trucking along.

films.title: First Daughter
films.note: President dad, first of a loose trilogy of supportive girl-dad turns in family comedies.

films.title: Herbie: Fully Loaded
films.note: Race-car dad. Amanda had forgotten how precipitous the 2000s drop-off was; this is a landmark of it.

films.title: Cars
films.note: Chick Hicks, the mean green rival in the three-way finish. Sean saw it for the first time at a playdate with his kid.

films.title: The Merry Gentleman
films.note: The first of two sad-hitman dramas he directed and starred in; he stepped in to direct after the original director's appendix burst ten days before shooting.

films.title: Post Grad
films.note: The third supportive girl-dad turn, the Alexis Bledel post-Gilmore Girls vehicle where he's just the encouraging father.

films.title: Toy Story 3
films.note: Ken. A supporting scene-stealer in a giant hit that Griffin took as a sign his Keaton stock would finally pay off.

films.title: The Other Guys
films.note: Ferrell and McKay just let him be funny, and he's remarkable: five lights-out scenes. The moment Griffin thought he was fully back.

films.title: Birdman
films.note: The comeback role, and his only Oscar nomination, which he loses to Eddie Redmayne (to the hosts, an Oscar travesty). Both find the movie built on a foundation of bullshit: its premise that no one takes this guy seriously falls apart because Keaton is too inherently intelligent to read as a dumb action hunk.

films.title: Need for Speed
films.note: One of the thankless supporting roles he was doing mere months before Birdman, filed under the stuff we can ignore.

films.title: RoboCop
films.note: The other thankless 2014 IP role, insane to place months before an Oscar nomination.

films.title: Minions
films.note: He voices Walter Nelson, one of two men named Walter he played in 2015.

films.title: Spotlight
films.note: Robby Robinson, the Globe editor and self-described 'player-coach.' Pure craft, all the tics stripped away: the 'that was me' moment is lights-out and devastating. Star of two straight Best Picture winners; the Lincoln slot.

films.title: The Founder
films.note: His Ray Kroc; a January-dumped movie that has weirdly grown for Griffin on rewatch. The low end of the Moneyball-adjacent men-and-their-demons genre, hobbled by John Lee Hancock's inert direction.

films.title: Spider-Man: Homecoming
films.note: Adrian Toomes / the Vulture, widely agreed to be one of the few MCU villains who is both operatic and grounded: just a guy who built something, no superpowers.

films.title: Dumbo
films.note: 'Business Beetlejuice': a sadistic white-haired mogul, basically evil Walt Disney, in Tim Burton's real-time existential crisis about being commodified by a major studio. Sean saw it at the El Capitan and found it bad.

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Ramsey Clark. Originally in line for the bigger Kunstler part (which went to Rylance) before scheduling shook out; his eight minutes are the best stretch of the movie.

films.title: The Protégé
films.note: Second lead to Maggie Q in a Martin Campbell thriller more or less built around how sexy he is at this age: she's hired to kill him but he's too hot. It does not work.

films.title: Worth
films.note: The Netflix-dumped 9/11 compensation-fund drama; Griffin calls it rock solid and Keaton's performance really good.

films.title: The Little Things
films.note: The most extreme case of John Lee Hancock inertia: where it ends feels like where the movie should have started.

films.title: Morbius
films.note: His Adrian Toomes cashes in for the end credits; nobody holds it against him, the way nothing sticks to him anymore.

films.title: Minions: The Rise of Gru
films.note: Named as the only Despicable Me-universe film Amanda has seen; occasion for a Minions-are-cinema debate.

films.title: The Flash
films.note: His Batman returns; he's the one who came out of the movie with a little heat and a bump rather than dragged down by it.

films.title: Knox Goes Away
films.note: His second sad-hitman movie as director-star: John Knox, a hitman with rapid-onset dementia who just becomes more evil. A strange, cruel choice that doesn't really work; even in press Keaton was passive about directing it.

films.title: Goodrich
films.note: Hallie Meyers-Shyer's film with Mila Kunis as his grown daughter: old-man Mr. Mom, a bad dad turned reluctant single father to young kids. Coming in six weeks; Sean's presumptive favorite of the year regardless of quality.

films.title: Rabbit Test
films.note: His film debut, a non-speaking role in Joan Rivers's comedy about a pregnant Billy Crystal, long before Junior.

films.title: Touch and Go
films.note: A hockey drama so slight that calling it bad gives it too much attention.

films.title: The Squeeze
films.note: One of the most bizarre studio films Sean has ever seen: an artist tangled in a criminal conspiracy, shot like an INXS video, impossible to follow scene to scene.

films.title: The Dream Team
films.note: Part of the post-Beetlejuice run that made his Batman casting look, on paper, like a mistake.

films.title: Quicksand
films.note: A direct-to-video-feeling thriller with Michael Caine, filed among the depressing 2000s IMDb credits.

films.title: The Last Time
films.note: Another of the barely-there 2000s credits, opposite Brendan Fraser.

segments.heading: The Michael Keaton Movie Mount Rushmore

films.title: Batman
films.note: George Washington: the biggest brand reach, the blockbuster that started everything, and the one time all of culture was looking at him.

films.title: Mr. Mom
films.note: Thomas Jefferson: less about greatness than influence, the first real leading-man role, an idea that persists as a concept and a way of life.

films.title: Beetlejuice
films.note: Teddy Roosevelt: unusual, fearless, and hard to pin to one lane; less screen time, more influence.

films.title: Spotlight
films.note: Abraham Lincoln: the restrained, steady, all-craft late-career peak; the bookend of the elder-statesman era.

segments.heading: Jason Schwartzman and Nathan Silver on 'Between the Temples'
segments.guest: Jason Schwartzman and Nathan Silver on
segments.note: the star and director, in conversation with Sean

films.title: Between the Temples
films.note: Silver's bigger, crew-having comedy about a grieving cantor who can't sing (Schwartzman) and his late-in-life bat mitzvah student (Carole Kane, cast during a feverish COVID night in Paris). Written from a 60-page 'scriptment' (between a treatment and a script, dialogue suggested not separated), with most lines built during an 18-day shoot. The climactic dinner scene, shot over two nights across three competing versions of the dialogue, is the work Silver is proudest of.

films.title: Cutting My Mother
films.note: The documentary Silver made about his mother after cutting her from an earlier film; making it, he learned she was taking a late-in-life bat mitzvah class, the seed of Between the Temples. Schwartzman watched it as a way of meeting Silver before they worked together.

films.title: Soft in the Head
films.note: Silver's third feature, cited as the moment an actor's on-the-fly choice (one performer dragged back into a scene) upended the plan and taught him what improvisation could do for a movie.

films.title: Harold and Maude
films.note: The May-December template a producer invoked when he pitched the story; Silver's co-writer C. Mason Wells hates it (thinks it's cloying), which made him the right partner to build something new.

films.title: The Strange Monsieur Victor
films.note: Silver's last-great-thing: a Jean Grémillon film he found on Criterion, about a man who lets another go to prison in his place and then must live with the guilt.

films.title: Challengers
films.note: Schwartzman's last-great-thing: he saw it alone in a theater while working in Atlanta and came away thrilled by the acting, the writing, and how hard it must be to shoot tennis.

referenced.group: The 2015 Best Actor field, and Oscar what-ifs
